parts.label: Part 1
parts.instructions: Read the text and choose the correct answer (A, B or C).
parts.passage: The rise of recommendation algorithms has quietly reshaped the way we read, watch and shop. Every time a platform suggests an article we might enjoy or a product we did not know we wanted, it draws on vast quantities of data about our past behaviour. Defenders of these systems argue that they save us time and expose us to options we would never have stumbled upon by ourselves. Critics, however, raise a subtler objection. By relentlessly showing us more of what we already like, such algorithms tend to enclose us in a comfortable bubble, gradually narrowing the range of ideas and experiences we encounter. Over time, the very tool designed to broaden our choices may end up shrinking them. What makes the debate particularly difficult is that the harm, if it exists, is rarely dramatic; it accumulates slowly and almost invisibly, one recommendation at a time. Few users notice the moment their feed stopped surprising them. Some researchers therefore call for greater transparency, urging companies to explain, at least in broad terms, why a given item appears. Others place the responsibility on individuals, insisting that a healthy dose of curiosity — deliberately seeking out unfamiliar voices and opinions — remains the best defence. What both camps share is the conviction that we should not surrender our judgement entirely to a system whose goals, however convenient, are not necessarily our own.

items.question: What is the subtle objection that critics raise?

options.text: That the algorithms are too slow to be useful.
That they gradually narrow the range of ideas we encounter.
That they recommend products that are too expensive.

items.question: Why is the debate described as particularly difficult?

options.text: Because the possible harm accumulates slowly and almost invisibly.
Because the algorithms never make mistakes.
Because users always notice when their feed changes.

items.question: Critics argue that recommendation algorithms can narrow the range of ideas we encounter.

parts.label: Part 2
parts.instructions: Read the article extract and answer the question.
parts.passage: Review: Fitness Tracking Apps. The reviewer praises the app's clear layout but criticises the fact that it collects far more personal data than most users expect. Although the sleep-tracking feature is accurate, the terms and conditions are difficult to understand.

parts.label: Part 1
parts.instructions: Read the transcript and choose the best answer.
parts.transcript:
— Have you printed the documents for the conference in Vienna?
— Almost. The flight is confirmed, but the hotel was full. I booked another one near the station.
— Fine, we'll meet at the airport tomorrow morning.

items.question: What is the main topic of the conversation?

parts.label: Part 2
parts.transcript:
Presenter: Can individual choices really fight climate change?
Scientist: They help, but systemic policy is essential. Governments must invest in renewable energy and public transport.
Presenter: Are younger voters willing to pay more?
Scientist: Surveys show growing support among younger voters.

parts.task: Write a formal email (about 100 words) complaining about a delayed delivery and requesting a specific solution.
